name: Poems for the Becoming-Beggar
description: A small, growing collection of poems for contemplative practice — drawn from retreat, from daughterhood, from the cracks where ordinary certainty has given way. Each card holds one poem. Draw a card and let the poem say what it says. Intended as a shared grammar: contributions welcome from practitioners whose poems belong here.

attribution.source_author: Multiple contributors — each poem attributed per item
attribution.note: Each poem is contributed by its author under CC BY-SA 4.0. Attribution preserved per item. Working title after Laura's poem 'Let yourself become a beggar' — the author may rename once the collection takes its final shape.

sections.Poem:
Let yourself become a beggar
A mendicant
An ignorant and wandering fool
Who knows nothing

And needs everything

Let your defenses be shattered,
Let your walls come crumbling down.

Your competency is what stands between you

And all you long for.
sections.The poet's note: I shared this poem on retreat and share it again with you now. I keep reminding myself that not knowing and not being capable leaves room for grace.
sections.What this poem teaches:
Competency is not a neutral quality. Competency is a wall. The things you most long for — grace, presence, being met, being held by what is larger than you — arrive in the gap competency closes. The practice is not to become incompetent; the practice is to let the competence you have stop functioning as a defense against what wants to reach you.

Beggardom is not a state of lack. Beggardom is a posture: I know nothing, I need everything, and I am available to be given what I cannot produce myself. This is the posture the contemplative traditions have been asking of practitioners for millennia, and it is the posture modern life trains out of us most thoroughly.
sections.A prompt for drawing this card: Where in your life is your competence standing between you and what you long for? Not: where are you incompetent? But: where has your capacity to handle, to manage, to solve, to perform become the exact thing that keeps you from being reached?

attribution.author: Laura Jarrait TI
attribution.context: Shared at a Śaiva Tantra retreat, California 2026; shared again in the post-retreat integration group on April 24, 2026
attribution.license: CC BY-SA 4.0, with the poet's permission to include

items.name: A Song That Loves You Back

sections.Poem:
The crash, the crush, that awful sound —
it planted something in the ground.
And what grew up between the cracks
is this: a song that loves you back.
sections.The poet's note: I wrote it a year or two ago, after a day that went badly in a small street-level way — a truck came too soon, a pile of things we had meant to give away went instead to a crushing place, and my daughter screamed down the block after it until the driver stopped, though by then there was nothing he could do. We sat on the curb together. I could not fix what had happened. I could only hold her. I wrote the song to try to find a shape for what had happened. The shape is not an argument. The shape is a refrain. I have been singing it to her for long enough that it has become, in its small way, what I mean when I mean practice.
sections.What this poem teaches:
The composting chapter. The crash is real. The crush is real. The ground receives what lands in it. The cracks are not where the story ends; the cracks are where the different growth becomes possible. What grows through the cracks is often small and unshowy. A song for a child. A book that took too long. A tool that was made quieter and not abandoned. A platform that was built more carefully than it was first imagined. A reading practice that became, over time, a way of standing.

The song that loves you back is what the composting was always for.
sections.A prompt for drawing this card: What has crashed or been crushed in your life recently, that you have not yet let compost? The question is not what to do about the loss. The question is what, very slowly, is starting to grow between the cracks the loss left behind.

attribution.author: PlayfulProcess
attribution.context: Written after a curb-side moment with the author's daughter and a truck that came too soon. Included in Chapter 12 (Composting) of 'The Axiom Beneath the Ground.'
